artifacts.content:
Hi Patty,

Finally sitting down for a minute now that Chloe’s spelling words are (mostly) done and Ethan’s pretending to study math. I just wanted to say thank you again for listening on Saturday when I was so wound up about the dog.

I still can’t shake how bad the night of 1/15 was. Once Ranger started up around 11:30, it felt like he barked straight past 1 a.m. I could hear him pacing along the fence by our bedroom wall, and by the time it finally went quiet I was so wired and angry I couldn’t fall back asleep. Michael and I ended up snapping at each other in our room at 7428, and then it spilled into an argument in front of the kids when Ethan came in complaining he was exhausted. Not my finest parenting moment.

After we got them back to bed, I actually wrote in my journal that I was worried we might end up needing marriage counseling at St. John’s Counseling Center if this keeps going the way it has. It scared me a little to see that in my own handwriting, but that’s honestly how frayed I felt at 2 a.m.

I know you and your mom are dealing with the same thing on your side of Ranger’s yard at 7432, so it helps to know we’re not crazy. I really, really appreciate you backing us up and just being a friendly face when I’m out there in my robe at midnight looking like a zombie.

If you and Milo want to walk with Daisy and me this weekend (weather permitting), I could use the fresh air and a break from thinking about barking. Maybe Saturday around 3?

Love,
Laura

Subject: Rough day at the clinic

Hey Patty,

Catching up on my lunch break in the tiny break room before the phones start ringing off the hook again. Today’s not as bad, but it reminded me of how things went right before Christmas.

Do you remember 12/22? That was the Monday after one of those really early-morning barking nights. I was at the front desk here at Springfield Family Medical Clinic, trying to check in a family, and all of a sudden I got so dizzy and anxious I thought I might pass out. My hands were shaking while I was verifying their insurance, and I kept second-guessing every date of birth I typed.

Dr. Karen Patel happened to walk by and noticed I was pale and breathing weird. She pulled me into her office, had me sit down, and brought me some water. After a few minutes she said she was concerned it might be anxiety or even a mild panic thing from all the chronic sleep loss I’ve been talking about, and she told me to go home for the rest of the day. I felt guilty leaving early, but honestly I was relieved.

Karen also suggested I call my primary care doctor and at least talk about what’s been going on—between Ranger barking half the night, the kids being up, and me making more little mistakes with charts. I stuck a reminder in my calendar to call but, of course, life and soccer practice and art club got in the way.

Anyway, today’s better—just the usual grumpy patients and printer jams. But thinking about that day made me realize I probably shouldn’t keep ignoring it.

Want to grab coffee after work one day this week?

Laura

Subject: Fwd: Note from Ethan’s math/science teacher

Hi Mike,

Forwarding you the email from Ms. Alvarez at Washington Middle School that came in on 12/22 about Ethan. I know you were already out on your route when it hit my inbox.

She wrote that his grades in math and science have dropped since around November and asked if there was anything going on at home that might explain why he seems so tired and distracted in class. She said he actually nodded off during science once last week and that he’s been slower turning in assignments.

I emailed her back during my break at the clinic and explained about the neighbor’s dog—how Ranger’s been barking late at night and sometimes really early in the morning, and how Ethan has been waking up multiple times. I told her about that Sunday before Christmas when the barking started around 5:20 a.m. and went almost to 6:00, and both kids were zombies at church and the rest of the day.

I also told her we’re trying to be consistent with Ethan’s bedtime and screen time, and that we’re working on the barking situation from our end. She seemed understanding and said she’d keep an eye on him and let us know if things get worse.

Can you talk to Ethan tonight after dinner, just gently, about staying on top of his homework even when he’s tired? I don’t want him to feel like he’s in trouble, but I also don’t want this to snowball as he gets closer to high school.

Love,
Laura

——— Forwarded message ———
From: Ms. Alvarez
Subject: Concerns re: Ethan’s recent performance
Date: 2025-12-22
(To: us)

[Email body omitted here, see above summary.]

metadata.subject: Fwd: Note from Ethan’s math/science teacher

artifacts.content:
Personal health / sleep notes – for my own reference

August 2025 – Saw Dr. Melissa Crane at Springfield Women’s Health about perimenopause stuff (hot flashes, trouble falling asleep even on quiet nights). She prescribed 0.5 mg lorazepam tablets “as needed” for the really bad insomnia nights, with strict instructions not to take them regularly.

At first I hardly used them. Maybe one half-tablet (0.25 mg) here and there when my mind wouldn’t shut off after a long day at the clinic.

Since Ranger showed up next door and the barking started getting bad, I’ve slipped into using them more often than I’m comfortable with. On 2026-01-10, after he barked on and off from about 11 p.m. to nearly 1, I finally got up, took half of one of the 0.5 mg pills with water in the kitchen, and read my mystery book on the couch until it kicked in. Slept maybe four hours straight, which felt like a miracle.

Did the same thing on 2026-01-15 after that awful night when Michael and I argued in our bedroom at 7428 and the kids saw us snapping at each other. I waited until everyone was in bed, then took another half-tablet because I was shaking and couldn’t stop replaying everything.

I don’t want to get dependent on these, especially with having to be sharp at Springfield Family Medical Clinic (DOBs, insurance, etc.). Need to:

• Call PCP (Dr. Lewis) about safer long-term options for sleep/anxiety.
• Ask Dr. Crane at next annual if she’s okay with how I’ve been using the lorazepam.
• Look into non-medication ideas: better bedtime routine, maybe counseling at St. John’s Counseling Center if things don’t settle down.

metadata.title: Sleep + medication log
metadata.location: Henderson home at 7428 Willow Bend Drive

Notes:

Use my break to finally call Dr. Lewis’s office about:

1. Episode on 2025-12-22 at Springfield Family Medical Clinic – got so dizzy and anxious at the front desk that Dr. Karen Patel pulled me into her office, gave me water, and told me to go home early. She specifically mentioned possible anxiety or panic tied to chronic sleep loss and said I should talk to my primary care doctor.

2. Ongoing sleep disruption from neighbor’s dog (Ranger) barking late at night and early morning along our bedroom wall at 7428 Willow Bend. Mention worst nights: 2026-01-10 and 2026-01-15.

3. Current coping: taking half of a 0.5 mg lorazepam tablet (0.25 mg) that Dr. Melissa Crane at Springfield Women’s Health originally prescribed in August 2025 for perimenopause-related insomnia. I’ve started using it on the really bad barking nights just to get a few hours of rest, and I’m worried about relying on it.

4. Ask whether counseling (e.g., St. John’s Counseling Center in Springfield) might be helpful if this is sliding into anxiety, and whether I should track episodes (dizziness, racing heart, arguments with Michael in front of kids) in a notebook.

Remember to have list of current meds handy and my work schedule so we can book an in-person appointment if needed.

artifacts.content:
Thread: Call with Mark about the barking

2026-01-05, 8:52 PM

Laura: Hey, just wanted to let you know I talked to Mark a little bit ago while you were putting Chloe’s art stuff away.

Michael: Our Mark? Or your cousin Mark?

Laura: Cousin Mark Reynolds, from Springfield PD. I called his personal cell around 8:30 while I was in our bedroom at 7428, just to get some informal advice about Ranger.

Michael: What did he say?

Laura: I told him how bad the barking’s been the last few months and about the late nights (like New Year’s and that 1/2 early morning). I asked what our options are and if we should start recording it on my phone in case the Millers ever try to say we’re harassing them.

Michael: And?

Laura: He said as long as we’re just documenting what’s actually happening in our own yard/house, we’re fine. He actually suggested we start saving a few recordings, especially on the worst nights, and jot down dates/times.

Michael: Ok, that makes me feel better.

Laura: Me too. I was worried it would somehow look like we’re the problem. He also said if it keeps up at this level, we shouldn’t feel bad about talking to someone about the stress – like counseling at St. John’s or through our doctor – especially with how it’s affecting the kids and my sleep.

Michael: Good to know. Thanks for calling him.

Laura: Of course. Let’s start using my phone tonight if Ranger starts up again.

metadata.title: Text thread with Michael – call to Mark

artifacts.content:
Subject: Catching up / I might need to borrow your ear

Hi Jen,

I finally have a quiet half-hour while Ethan works on his science project and Chloe is drawing yet another unicorn. Figured it was a good time to actually write a real email instead of just liking your photos.

Life here in Springfield is its usual circus. The kids are good overall, but tired. Ethan’s been struggling a bit at Washington Middle School since around November—his math and science grades dipped, and on 12/22 his teacher, Ms. Alvarez, emailed us to ask if there were problems at home because he’s been droopy in class and even nodded off once. I had to explain that the neighbor’s German Shepherd mix (“Ranger”) has been barking half the night along our bedroom wall at 7428 and waking everyone up.

The lack of sleep is catching up with me too. Back on 12/22, I actually had to leave Springfield Family Medical Clinic early because I got so dizzy and anxious at the front desk that our supervising physician, Dr. Karen Patel, pulled me aside and told me to go home and talk to my primary care doc about possible anxiety or panic from chronic sleep loss.

On top of that, on the worst nights—like 1/10 and 1/15—I’ve started taking half of one of the 0.5 mg lorazepam pills my OB/GYN, Dr. Melissa Crane at Springfield Women’s Health, prescribed last August for perimenopause insomnia. Just 0.25 mg so I can get a few hours, but I don’t love that I’m depending on it.

Michael and I even snapped at each other in front of the kids after that 1/15 barking marathon. I caught myself thinking, “If this keeps up, we’re going to end up in marriage counseling at St. John’s Counseling Center,” which was a bit of a wake-up call.

Otherwise, we’re okay—still doing tacos on Tuesdays and trying to stretch the grocery budget. How are you holding up?

Love,
Laura

metadata.subject: Catching up / I might need to borrow your ear
